channel.title: DutchNews.nl
channel.description: DutchNews.nl brings daily news from The Netherlands in English

item.title: Eindhoven students build world’s first solar-powered ambulance
item.description:
Eindhoven students build world’s first solar-powered ambulance

July 21, 2026 Louis Gore-Langton

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Students at Eindhoven University of Technology have unveiled what they say is the world’s first solar-powered ambulance, designed to bring medical care to remote regions where poor roads and a lack of fuel or electricity put healthcare out of reach.

Stella Juva, presented on Tuesday, was developed in a year by Solar Team Eindhoven, a group of 23 students. Solar panels on the roof power both the motor and the medical equipment on board, and on a sunny day the vehicle is expected to cover 715 kilometres without needing any charging infrastructure.

But how does a solar vehicle fare in one of Europe’s cloudiest countries? “The Dutch weather, to say the least, is not Kenya’s,” team manager Mathijs van Gerven told DutchNews after the launch. The ambulance’s 50 kWh battery stores solar energy so it can keep powering its equipment, and its own motors, without interruption, he explained.

Unlike a traditional ambulance, which transports patients to hospital, Stella Juva is designed to bring care to people, allowing healthcare providers to carry out blood tests, screen for diseases such as tuberculosis and malaria, administer vaccinations and perform pregnancy ultrasounds.

The students developed the vehicle with international health organisations including Amref Health Africa, formerly known as the Flying Doctors. “In remote areas, healthcare workers sometimes travel great distances by bicycle, and reliable energy is not a given,” Femke Maurits of Amref’s Dutch branch said in a statement.

Energy is essential, she said, for keeping vaccines cooled and running medical equipment.

Kenya test
In August the team will take Stella Juva to Kenya, starting with a kick-off at Amref International University attended by officials from the Dutch embassy and Kenyan diplomats. The vehicle will then be tested at two field locations, where healthcare scenarios, such as the treatment of tuberculosis patients, will be simulated.

“Three of our team members have been chosen to drive Stella Juva – they were the first to test her,” Van Gerven told us. “We will see who gets to drive the first Kenyan metres, but I reckon they will all want to go first.”

Afterwards, the ambulance will tour events across the Netherlands, and possibly Europe, to encourage companies and healthcare providers to take the concept further. For production at scale, the team is looking to industry.

“If 23 students are able to do this in just one year, think about what is possible if technological companies and social organisations collaborate,” Van Gerven said.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Lorries ignoring Merwedebrug ban face €500 fines
item.description:
Lorries ignoring Merwedebrug ban face €500 fines

July 21, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Roads agency Rijkswaterstaat is installing traffic cameras on the damaged Merwedebrug near Gorinchem to enforce a ban on lorries, with fines of up to €500 for drivers who ignore it.

The bridge on the A27 has been closed since Saturday to freight traffic after an inspection found the bridge’s steel was weaker than expected in several places, and that it could no longer guarantee the structure would hold under heavy traffic.

The roads agency issued an urgent appeal to trucks to avoid using the bridge but many are still driving over it.

An NOS reporter on the scene counted dozens of trucks in half an hour, including vehicles with Dutch license plates.

The new ban will now be enforced via cameras which will automatically read the license plates. Enforcement will be done together with the police and the public prosecution service. Fines can be as high as €500.

Cars, vans, motorbikes, buses and emergency vehicles can still use the Merwedebrug. Freight traffic is being diverted via the A16 and the A2.
item.dc:creator: Molly Quell

item.title: Deaths linked to online medicines prompt national study
item.description:
Deaths linked to online medicines prompt national study

July 21, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The addiction research institute Trimbos is starting an investigation into “self-medication”, people buying medicines online without consulting a doctor, it has told broadcaster NOS.

The study has been commissioned by health minister Sophie Hermans, who is worried about injuries and even deaths linked to medicines sold online.

Drugs bought online can be dangerous, according to Laura Smit-Rigter, national coordinator of drug monitoring and policy at Trimbos. “It could involve counterfeit medication,” she told NOS. “In that case, the dosage or composition of the product could be completely different. And that can be life-threatening.”

In 2025, two men who ran a webshop called Funcaps, which sold fake and illegal medicines to the general public, appeared in court in connection with the deaths of at least 27 people.

Funcaps sold a range of substances, among them bromazolam, a sedative, O-DSMT, an opioid painkiller, and 2F-ketamine, an anaesthetic. The products came with no package inserts or usage instructions, and sales were pushed with discount deals and sample packs.

Trimbos wants to find out how many people are buying medicines online and why they turn to the internet instead of going to their doctor.
item.dc:creator: Molly Quell

item.title: Big firms hired 13,500 refugees and asylum seekers last year
item.description:
Big firms hired 13,500 refugees and asylum seekers last year

July 21, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Large companies in the Netherlands hired 13,500 refugees and asylum seekers last year, in an effort to plug staff shortages in sectors such as logistics, hospitality and technical trades.

The figures come from Tent Nederland, a coalition of 51 major employers including Philips, IKEA, ABN Amro, Unilever, Arcadis and Randstad, and were reported by NU.nl. The jobs include both permanent positions and work via staffing agencies.

Tent Nederland was set up in April 2025 as the Dutch arm of the Tent Partnership for Refugees, a global network of large companies founded by Chobani boss Hamdi Ulukaya.

The organisation puts employers in direct contact with refugees, partly through regional job fairs organised with local councils and the refugee settlement agency COA.

Companies are finding it easier to reach refugees, director Hélène van Melle told NU.nl, including asylum seekers, who have been able to work year round since a court scrapped a 24-week annual limit at the end of 2023.

“Employers initially had great difficulty getting in contact with these people,” she said. “But within a year we are seeing that companies are open to taking on asylum seekers.”

Government target
Some firms are recruiting refugees by their own initiative. Construction group Heijmans, which is not a Tent Nederland member, has taken on 60 refugees with residence permits over the past two years.

The hirings fit with government plans to get 75,000 more refugees and promising asylum seekers into paid work by the end of 2030. Work and participation minister Thierry Aartsen wants 60% of new arrivals to have a proper job within five years of being given a residence permit.

Refugee employment has long lagged the rest of the workforce, although CBS figures published in May show new arrivals are finding work faster than a decade ago.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Fake emergency calls send armed arrest teams to teenagers’ doors
item.description:
Fake emergency calls send armed arrest teams to teenagers’ doors

July 21, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Public prosecutors and police are warning about the rise of “swatting” in the Netherlands, in which hoax emergency reports are used to send heavily armed arrest teams to an unsuspecting and innocent person’s home.

The phenomenon, which originated in the US, “is now happening more often here too, especially among young people,” public prosecutor Linda van den Oever told broadcaster NOS.

Swatting can be an act of revenge after an online row, a way of shocking people or a means of coercion, the public prosecution service (OM) said. Contact often begins on gaming or chat platforms, where trust is built up before sensitive information, in extreme cases nude photographs, is used to put victims under pressure.

In May, an arrest team turned up at night at the home of a 17-year-old girl after fake reports were made to police when she refused to send nude photos, she told the AD. Her address had been shared in a chat group after she refused to carry out orders.

Threats of swatting are increasingly common in so-called Com groups – short for “community”, online networks in which violence is glorified, the OM said.

The threats are used to coerce victims, while carrying out a swatting can serve as an initiation: “If you send a SWAT team after someone, you are allowed into the group,” Van den Oever said.

Digital community police officer Malek Kariebo told NOS making a false report is a criminal offence and takes police away from real emergencies.

“You know nothing about it,” he said, “and suddenly an arrest team is standing at your door, wrongly.” Police assess whether reports are genuine, but a claim that there is a firearm in a house has to be taken seriously.

There are no exact figures on how often swatting happens, but several cases are under way and a growing number of suspects in the Netherlands have been identified, the OM said.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: They are off: 108th edition of the Nijmeegse Vierdaagse begins
item.description:
They are off: 108th edition of the Nijmeegse Vierdaagse begins

July 21, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The 108th edition of the Nijmeegse Vierdaagse, or four-day walk, kicked off at 4am on Tuesday.

More than 45,000 walkers from more than 70 countries are taking part in this year’s event, with marchers covering up to 50 kilometres a day.

The first day takes participants across the Waal Bridge into the Betuwe region, into the town of Elst. It is known as “Blue Tuesday” and the centre of the Elst is decorated in blue with walkers welcomed by bands and spectators.

Each day, the marchers will take different route through Gelderland, Brabant and Limburg. They can choose to walk 30, 40 or 50 kilometres a day.

The Vierdaagse was first held in 1909, when 306 mostly military participants set out from 10 army garrisons around the country, an initiative of the Dutch League for Physical Education amid fears that the rise of motorised transport would erode soldiers’ fitness.

The event moved permanently to Nijmegen in 1925 and has been skipped only in 1914 and 1915, during the Second World War and in the coronavirus years. The 2006 edition was called off after the first day, when two walkers died in extreme heat.

Marchers will get lucky with the weather, which is forecast to be between 20° and 22° degrees in the afternoon, slightly lower than average. The next four days will mostly be sunny but with occasional showers on Wednesday and Thursday.

The age of participants has been steadily declining. For years, walkers were mostly 60 plus but this year 20.5% of walkers are in their 20s. They only accounted for 7.5% of marchers in 2016.

The march ends on Friday in Nijmegen. At the finish, the walkers are welcomed by thousands of spectators and showered with flowers.
item.dc:creator: Molly Quell

item.title: Ex-police officers seek recognition after apology to Moluccans
item.description:
Ex-police officers seek recognition after apology to Moluccans

July 20, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

A group of around 100 former police officers have asked for official recognition of what they went through during the violent confrontations with young Moluccans in the 1970s, a month after prime minister Rob Jetten apologised on behalf of the government for the treatment of the Moluccan community.

Four retired officers filed a petition with Assen city council and the police recognition foundation on behalf of the group, the Volkskrant reported. They argue that the government ordered them to enforce a hardline policy against the Moluccan community which made the situation worse, and which the state has now admitted was wrong.

“We want it recognised that the hard policy also caused suffering for the police,” retired officer Ed Jissink (76) told the paper, describing a period in which officers’ homes were firebombed and a patrol car was riddled with bullets in an ambush. Some colleagues later developed PTSD or took their own lives, he told current affairs programme Nieuwsuur.

Broken promises 
Some 12,500 Moluccans – around 3,500 soldiers who had fought on the Dutch side in the Indonesian war of independence, together with their families – were brought to the Netherlands in 1951 and housed in camps, including the former concentration camps Westerbork and Vught, on the promise that their stay would be temporary.

When it became clear the Netherlands would not support an independent Moluccan republic, frustration among the second generation boiled over into violence, including the 1977 train hijacking at De Punt, in which two hostages and six hijackers died, and the 1978 hostage-taking at the provincial government building in Assen, which left two people dead.

Police recognition 
Jissink said his own view began to change in 1974, when he saw how stateless Moluccans were treated. He later refused to take part in a training exercise practising live fire on demonstrators, which the police subsequently ended. He hopes the lesson will be remembered now that many politicians are again calling for tougher treatment of some migrant groups.

John Wattilete, president of the Moluccan government in exile, said the officers were right to raise the issue “without diminishing Moluccan suffering”, since they too had been used to manage what was really a political problem.

He is calling for a full investigation into the government’s role in the period. The national police force said the officers’ request deserved a serious response.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Picnic makes first operating profit in NL
item.description:
Picnic makes first operating profit in NL

July 20, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Online supermarket Picnic made a profit in the Netherlands for the first time last year, 10 years after its launch, according to annual figures published on Monday and reported by the Financieele Dagblad.

The Dutch arm booked an underlying operating profit of €4 million, but heavy spending on new distribution centres in Germany pushed the group as a whole to a net loss of €272 million, slightly more than the year before.

The number of customers across the Netherlands, Germany and France grew by 700,000 to 3.6 million.

Picnic, which delivers in three-quarters of the Netherlands, is still in dispute with unions FNV and CNV over which collective labour agreement should cover its delivery workers. It lost a court case last year over unpaid evening and weekend supplements and is negotiating a settlement while appealing to the Supreme Court.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Asylum agency claims €185m from hotel reception middleman
item.description:
Asylum agency claims €185m from hotel reception middleman

July 20, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The refugee settlement agency COA is claiming €184.7 million from René Derksen and his company LCHD, which arranged emergency accommodation for asylum seekers in hotels across the Netherlands, the Financieele Dagblad reported on Monday.

The agency has secured a claim against Derksen’s farmhouse in Didam, Gelderland, blocking any sale of the property. It is is one of the largest claims ever brought by a Dutch government body against a company.

COA confirmed to the paper it has started legal proceedings against LCHD and its directors over “a dispute about the execution of the assignment by LCHD and the termination of rental agreements”, but declined to comment further while the case is before the courts.

LCHD acted as COA’s exclusive intermediary for hotel accommodation from spring 2022, when the arrival of refugees from Ukraine left the agency scrambling for beds.

Over two and a half years, COA paid the company more than €500 million to book hotel rooms nationwide. FD reported earlier that Derksen paid himself more than €40 million in dividends over the period.

Derksen has been a suspect in a criminal investigation since 2023, alongside his former business partner Bob van der Valk, a member of the hotel family.

Prosecutors and the fiscal investigation service FIOD are looking into the two men’s cooperation and the money flows between them; both are suspected of, among other things, private-sector corruption.

Derksen has denied any wrongdoing, saying his prices were transparent and everyone involved knew LCHD was a commercial party making a “logical minimum margin”. LCHD has lodged a counter-claim against COA for what it calls unjustified breaches of contract.

The agency spent €2.5 billion on emergency accommodation in hotels and on cruise ships in 2024, against €1 billion on regular reception.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Major transport disruptions over A27 bridge safety warnings
item.description:
Major transport disruptions over A27 bridge safety warnings

July 20, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The Merwedebrug, which carries the A27 motorway over the river Merwede near Gorinchem, has been closed to all lorries since Saturday evening.

Roads agency Rijkswaterstaat said inspections found the bridge’s steel was weaker than expected in several places, and that it could no longer guarantee the structure would hold under heavy traffic.

Cars, vans, motorbikes, buses and emergency vehicles can still use it.

Lorries are being sent on long long detours as a result. It is not clear how long the closure will last.

Transport association TLN, which represents Dutch hauliers, said the impact was enormous. “Transport operators are already facing sharply rising costs,” the group said, pointing to a new lorry levy introduced on July 1 and higher diesel prices. “Extra detour kilometres only increase the financial pressure.”

The nearby Papendrechtsebrug closed last week for nine months of maintenance, cutting off one alternative route. The Merwedebrug was last shut to lorries in 2016, when cracks were found in its beams. That closure lasted nearly three months. A replacement bridge is due to be built starting this summer.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton
